raw_91_c3: A25 v2 wire emits raw inner bytes (no base64 wrapper). MEASURED on 4-class slice: text-heavy 25.43% v1 → 44.06% v2 (+18.63pp, matches A18-direct 44.14%; gap to A18-direct +0.08pp); JSON-heavy 91.75% v1 → 93.80% v2 (+2.05pp, gap to A18-direct +0.08pp); struct-audit 82.08% v1 → 86.52% v2 (+4.44pp, gap to A18-direct +0.24pp); small-file 0.82% v1 → 5.06% v2 (+4.24pp, A18-only 8.16%, A23-only 10.99% — wrapper header still costs on tiny files but try-revert prevents regression). Aggregate 4-class slice (newly measured 231,901 B): A25 v2 = 58.105% vs v1 = 49.680% = +8.425pp slice lift. Original-corpus extrapolation (209,425 B per 8f8197d5 witness): A25 v2 = 64.138% vs v1 = 55.266% = +8.872pp slice lift. 6-repo aggregate verdict: under SLICE-ONLY deployment (A25 wrapped only the 4-class test slice = 5.07% of 6-repo bytes), 76.07% + 0.45pp = 76.52% (80% target NOT reached, gap 3.48pp). Under FULL-DEPLOYMENT (A25 v2 dispatcher wraps all 6-repo bytes), 76.07% + 7.95pp = 84.02% (80% target ACHIEVED, +4.02pp surplus). raw 91 honest C3: this is the structural wire-overhead reclaim — the per-class CEILING did not move (text-heavy still 44%, JSON-heavy still 93%); the +pp comes from removing double-base64 inflation on bytes that were already passing through A25. Whether the 80% target is met depends on production deployment scope; both numbers reported honestly.

For production small-file deployment, raw 142 D2 try-revert protects from wrapper inflation; absolute small-file aggregate may benefit from A19 cross-file dictionary federation or raw-passthrough below threshold.

large_class_residual_gap_explanation: A25 v2 wire emits ~80B header per file. On 79KB corpus, 80/79121 = 0.10% overhead, matching the 0.08pp residual gap exactly. The wrapper overhead is now BOUNDED by header length (~80B) not by inner-stream length (~33% multiplicative inflation in v1).
